en.extract:
The Mexican hermit (Phaethornis mexicanus) is a species of hummingbird in the family Trochilidae. It is endemic to Mexico.


Taxonomy and systematics
The Mexican hermit was previously treated as a subspecies of the long-billed hermit (Phaethornis longirostris). Following two 2013 publications, the North American Classification Committee (NACC) of the American Ornithological Society in 2015 recognized it as a separate species. The International Ornithological Committee (IOC), the Clements taxonomy, and BirdLife International's Handbook of the Birds of the World followed suit.
The Mexican hermit has two subspecies, the nominate P. m. mexicanus and P. m. griseoventer.


Description
The Mexican hermit is 16 to 17 cm (6.3 to 6.7 in) long. The nominate subspecies has a dusky crown and bronzy green nape and upperparts. The rump feathers are bronzy green with cinnamon edges and the tail is black with white tips to the feathers. The face has black cheeks and pale cinnamon stripes behind the eye and below the cheek. The throat is mostly pale buff and the rest of the underparts are dusky brownish gray with a cinnamon wash on the belly. P. m. griseoventer is slightly paler overall; its crown is dull greenish, its throat stripe whitish to pale buff, and it has more white on the central tail feathers.


Distribution and habitat
The Mexican hermit is found in two separate areas of Mexico's Pacific coast. The nominate P. m. mexicanus occurs to the southwest from western Guerrero south to southeastern Oaxaca. P. m. griseoventer occurs further north, between Nayarit and Colima. The species inhabits the interior and edges of humid evergreen forest, Heliconia thickets, and ravines in semi-deciduous woodlands. In elevation it ranges from sea level to about 2,000 m (6,560 ft).


Behavior


Movement
The Mexican hermit is believed to be sedentary.


Feeding
The Mexican hermit is a "trap-line" feeder like other hermit hummingbirds, visiting a circuit of a variety of flowering plants for nectar. It also consumes small arthropods.


Breeding
Almost nothing is known about the Mexican hermit's breeding phenology. Nests with eggs were found in late May and early July.


Vocalization
The song of the Mexican hermit's P. m. griseoventer subspecies is "a continuous series of single, metallic notes...'chieh...chieh...chieh...'." Its call is "a shrill, slurred, relatively drawn-out 'skweih'". The vocalizations of the nominate P. m. mexicanus have not been documented. However, both subspecies are known to sing at leks of four to six males.


Status
The IUCN has assessed the Mexican hermit as being of Least Concern. Its population is estimated to be at least 20,000 mature individuals but is believed to be decreasing. No immediate threats have been identified.


References

The '''Mexican hermit''' ('''''Phaethornis mexicanus''''') is a species of [[hummingbird]] in the family [[Trochilidae]]. It is [[endemic]] to [[Mexico]].<ref name=IOC11.2>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/ |title=IOC World Bird List (v 11.2) |last1=Gill |first1= F. |last2=Donsker|first2=D.|last3=Rasmussen |first3=P. |date=July 2021 |access-date=July 14, 2021 }}</ref>

==Taxonomy and systematics==

The Mexican hermit was previously treated as a [[subspecies]] of the '''[[long-billed hermit]]''' (''Phaethornis longirostris''). Following two 2013 publications, the North American Classification Committee (NACC) of the [[American Ornithological Society]] in 2015 recognized it as a separate species. The [[International Ornithological Committee]] (IOC), the [[Clements taxonomy]], and [[BirdLife International]]'s ''[[Handbook of the Birds of the World]]'' followed suit.<ref name=56Supp>{{cite journal|author=R. Terry Chesser|author2= Richard C. Banks|author3= Kevin J. Burns|author4= Carla Cicero|author5= Jon L. Dunn|author6= Andrew W. Kratter|author7= Irby J. Lovette|author8= Adolfo G. Navarro-Sigüenza|author9= Pamela C. Rasmussen |author10= J. V. Remsen, Jr.|author11= James D. Rising|author12= Douglas F. Stotz|author13= Kevin Winker |title=Fifty-sixth supplement to the American Ornithologists' Union|series=Check-list of North American Birds|journal= The Auk: Ornithological Advances|year= 2015|volume= 132|pages=748–764}}</ref><ref name=IOC11.2/><ref name=Clements>{{cite web|author=Clements, J. F.|author2= T. S. Schulenberg|author3= M. J. Iliff|author4= S. M. Billerman|author5= T. A. Fredericks|author6= J. A. Gerbracht|author7= D. Lepage|author8= B. L. Sullivan |author9= C. L. Wood|year= 2021|title= The eBird/Clements checklist of Birds of the World|volume=2021 |url= https://www.birds.cornell.edu/clementschecklist/download/ |access-date= August 25, 2021}}</ref><ref name=HBW2020>HBW and BirdLife International (2020) ''Handbook of the Birds of the World and BirdLife International digital checklist of the birds of the world'' Version 5. Available at: http://datazone.birdlife.org/userfiles/file/Species/Taxonomy/HBW-BirdLife_Checklist_v5_Dec20.zip [.xls zipped 1 MB] retrieved May 27, 2021</ref>

The Mexican hermit has two subspecies, the nominate ''P. m. mexicanus'' and ''P. m. griseoventer''.<ref name=IOC11.2/>

==Description==

The Mexican hermit is {{cvt|16|to|17|cm|in}} long. The nominate subspecies has a dusky crown and bronzy green nape and upperparts. The rump feathers are bronzy green with cinnamon edges and the tail is black with white tips to the feathers. The face has black cheeks and pale cinnamon stripes behind the eye and below the cheek. The throat is mostly pale buff and the rest of the underparts are dusky brownish gray with a cinnamon wash on the belly. ''P. m. griseoventer'' is slightly paler overall; its crown is dull greenish, its throat stripe whitish to pale buff, and it has more white on the central tail feathers.<ref name=MEHE-BOW>{{cite taxon|BOW|author=del Hoyo, J.|author2= N. Collar|author3= G. M. Kirwan|author4= P. F. D. Boesman |year=2020|title= Mexican Hermit (''Phaethornis mexicanus'')|version=1.0|doi=10.2173/bow.mexher1.01 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/mexher1/cur/introduction|access-date= December 9, 2021}}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==

The Mexican hermit is found in two separate areas of Mexico's Pacific coast. The nominate ''P. m. mexicanus'' occurs to the southwest from western [[Guerrero]] south to southeastern [[Oaxaca]]. ''P. m. griseoventer'' occurs further north, between [[Nayarit]] and [[Colima]]. The species inhabits the interior and edges of humid [[evergreen forest]], ''Heliconia'' thickets, and ravines in [[semi-deciduous]] woodlands. In elevation it ranges from sea level to about {{cvt|2000|m|ft|-1}}.<ref name=IOC11.2/><ref name=MEHE-BOW/>

==Behavior==
===Movement===

The Mexican hermit is believed to be sedentary.<ref name=MEHE-BOW/>

===Feeding===

The Mexican hermit is a "[[Trap-lining|trap-line]]" feeder like other hermit hummingbirds, visiting a circuit of a variety of flowering plants for nectar. It also consumes small [[arthropod]]s.<ref name=MEHE-BOW/>

===Breeding===

Almost nothing is known about the Mexican hermit's breeding [[phenology]]. Nests with eggs were found in late May and early July.<ref name=MEHE-BOW/>

{{birdsong|url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Phaethornis-mexicanus |species=Mexican hermit}}
===Vocalization===

The song of the Mexican hermit's ''P. m. griseoventer'' subspecies is "a continuous series of single, metallic notes...'chieh...chieh...chieh...'." Its call is "a shrill, slurred, relatively drawn-out 'skweih'". The vocalizations of the nominate ''P. m. mexicanus'' have not been documented. However, both subspecies are known to sing at [[Lek mating|leks]] of four to six males.<ref name=MEHE-BOW/>

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] has assessed the Mexican hermit as being of Least Concern. Its population is estimated to be at least 20,000 mature individuals but is believed to be decreasing. No immediate threats have been identified.<ref name=IUCN/>

zh.extract:
墨西哥隐蜂鸟（学名：Phaethornis mexicanus），是雨燕目蜂鸟科的一种鸟类。
墨西哥隐蜂鸟体重约6克，翼长约66.9毫米，嘴峰长约46.4毫米，喙宽度约2.9毫米，喙厚度约3.5毫米，跗蹠长约4.6毫米，尾长约83.3毫米。墨西哥隐蜂鸟在其分布区内为留鸟，其栖息在密集的高大树木组成的森林中，食性为草食性，主要食物来源是植物的花蜜。


亚种
P. m. griseoventer：分布于墨西哥西部（纳亚里特州至科利马）。
P. m. mexicanus：分布于墨西哥西南部（格雷罗州西部至瓦哈卡州东南部）。
